_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against WHO 2023 Typhoid and Paratyphoid fever surveillance guidance, CDC Yellow Book 2024, and published literature on azithromycin and fluoroquinolone resistance in Salmonella Typhi/Paratyphi. Drug doses and treatment durations checked against up-to-date clinical guidelines.

h1Title: Paratyphoid Fever: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Paratyphoid fever is a systemic bacterial infection caused by Salmonella enterica serovars Paratyphi A, B, and C — close relatives of the Salmonella Typhi that causes typhoid fever. Like typhoid, paratyphoid spreads through contaminated food and water, produces a prolonged fever indistinguishable from typhoid without laboratory testing, and carries the risk of serious complications if untreated. The disease is endemic across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and parts of sub-Saharan Africa, where it imposes a heavy burden on children and young adults. Paratyphoid now outnumbers typhoid cases in several endemic regions as typhoid vaccination programs reduce Typhi incidence. Treatment is straightforward when antibiotic therapy is timely; resistance to first-line agents has become a growing challenge that shapes modern treatment algorithms worldwide.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 3-5 million cases annually globally; paratyphoid A now more common than typhoid in many endemic regions
keyStats.demographics: Children aged 5-15 years bear the highest burden in endemic areas; travelers to endemic zones of all ages are at risk
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in school-aged children and young adults in endemic regions
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 20-30 million cases of enteric fever (combined typhoid and paratyphoid) per year worldwide

definition: Paratyphoid fever (ICD-10: A01.1 for Paratyphi A; A01.2 for Paratyphi B; A01.3 for Paratyphi C) is an acute systemic enteric infection caused by the host-adapted Salmonella enterica serovars Paratyphi A, Paratyphi B (tartrate negative), and Paratyphi C. These organisms are genetically closely related to Salmonella Typhi but carry distinct virulence plasmids and express different O-antigens that define their serologic identity. Paratyphi A accounts for the majority of cases globally. Transmission occurs via the fecal-oral route — contaminated water, ice, raw produce washed in contaminated water, unpasteurized dairy, and foods prepared by asymptomatic carriers. After ingestion, the bacteria survive gastric acid, invade the intestinal mucosa, are phagocytosed by macrophages, and disseminate via the reticuloendothelial system to the liver, spleen, bone marrow, and gallbladder. The clinical syndrome of sustained bacteremic fever with relative bradycardia, abdominal symptoms, and a characteristic rose-spot rash develops over an incubation period of 6-30 days. The gallbladder serves as the reservoir for chronic carriage, which persists in approximately 1-4% of patients after clinical recovery. Paratyphoid fever is managed by infectious disease specialists, with primary care involved in travel medicine, occupational health, and post-treatment follow-up.

typesClassification.description: The dominant serovar causing paratyphoid globally, accounting for the majority of cases especially in South Asia. Clinically identical to typhoid fever; differentiation requires blood or bone marrow culture. Increasingly resistant to ciprofloxacin in South and Southeast Asia.

Causes paratyphoid B (also called S. Schottmuelleri). Associated with foodborne outbreaks in temperate regions and parts of Europe historically. Generally more responsive to antibiotics than Paratyphi A. Can cause gastroenteritis as a primary presentation in some cases.

Rare serovar; primarily reported in parts of sub-Saharan Africa and Asia. Can cause a more severe bacteremic picture in patients with underlying hemoglobinopathies such as sickle cell disease. Uncommon in travelers.

primarySymptoms: Prolonged sustained fever exceeding 39°C, typically rising in stepwise fashion over the first week and persisting without treatment for 3-4 weeks — the hallmark of untreated enteric fever.
Severe headache, often frontal or retro-orbital, present in the majority of patients during the first week of illness.
Abdominal pain and distension, with tenderness over the right upper quadrant where the liver and spleen are enlarging.
Constipation during the first week of illness in adults — a key early feature distinguishing enteric fever from many other causes of fever.
Diarrhea (sometimes described as pea-soup diarrhea) appearing in the second week in untreated patients, alternating with constipation in some cases.
Relative bradycardia — the pulse rate is lower than expected for the degree of fever, a classic finding in enteric fevers.
Rose spots — faint blanching salmon-colored macules on the trunk, appearing in crops during the second week of illness in roughly 30% of patients.
Hepatomegaly and splenomegaly — the liver and spleen enlarge as the reticuloendothelial system becomes populated with Salmonella-laden macrophages.
Cough and mild respiratory symptoms early in the course, which can mislead toward a primary respiratory diagnosis.
Generalized malaise, profound fatigue, and weakness that is disproportionate to the apparent severity of early physical findings.
Intestinal perforation presenting as sudden severe abdominal pain with peritoneal signs — a life-threatening complication of the third week in untreated disease.

Sustained bacteremia in the untreated patient, with bacteria seeding secondary sites including bones (osteomyelitis), joints (septic arthritis), meninges (bacterial meningitis), and heart valves (endocarditis).
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent fever above 38°C for 3 or more days following travel to an endemic region — even mild diarrhea or headache warrants testing for enteric fever.
Headache combined with fever and constipation in a returning traveler from South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, or Southeast Asia.
Any febrile illness in a household where a confirmed case of typhoid or paratyphoid has been identified — all household contacts should seek testing.
Abdominal symptoms (pain, distension, change in bowel habit) emerging in the second week of an unexplained febrile illness — possible intestinal involvement.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe abdominal pain with rigid abdomen and rebound tenderness — intestinal perforation is a medical emergency requiring immediate surgical evaluation.
High fever with neck stiffness, photophobia, and altered mental status — bacterial meningitis secondary to Salmonella requires urgent lumbar puncture and empiric antibiotics.
Septic shock with hypotension, tachycardia, and altered consciousness in the context of confirmed or suspected enteric fever.
Severe rectal bleeding or hematochezia — intestinal hemorrhage from Peyer's patch ulceration is a surgical emergency.
Fever unresponsive to appropriate antibiotics after 5-7 days — possible drug resistance requiring change in regimen and investigation for abscess or other complications.

causes.description: The most common route in endemic areas. Salmonella Paratyphi is shed in the feces of infected individuals and chronic carriers. In settings with inadequate water treatment and sewage infrastructure, contaminated drinking water sustains community transmission cycles. Even brief exposure to contaminated water used for drinking, ice, or brushing teeth during travel is sufficient for infection.

Raw or undercooked foods washed in contaminated water — particularly leafy greens, street foods, raw fruit, and vegetables — are common vehicles. Dairy products from unpasteurized sources, including milk and cheese, have been linked to paratyphoid outbreaks. Shellfish harvested from sewage-contaminated waters carry a particular risk.

Approximately 1-4% of people recovering from paratyphoid become chronic carriers, shedding bacteria in stool for over one year. The gallbladder is the primary reservoir. Chronic carriers are often unaware of their status and can transmit infection for decades. Food handlers who are carriers pose particular risks in communal dining settings.

causes.cause: Close household contact with an active case
causes.description: Household contacts of confirmed cases are at elevated risk and are candidates for post-exposure prophylaxis. The attack rate among household contacts in endemic settings can reach 10-15% without intervention. This route is less relevant for travelers but important in outbreak control.

Multidrug-resistant (MDR) Salmonella Paratyphi A has become widespread across South Asia, with resistance to ampicillin, chloramphenicol, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole now common. Fluoroquinolone resistance (via mutations in the quinolone resistance-determining region of the gyrA gene) is prevalent in South and Southeast Asia. Third-generation cephalosporins and azithromycin remain effective, though azithromycin resistance has been reported in Southeast Asia. This resistance landscape has significantly changed treatment protocols since the early 2000s.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to or residence in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: The Indian subcontinent (especially Nepal, Bangladesh, and India), Southeast Asia (Vietnam, Pakistan, Indonesia, Thailand), and sub-Saharan Africa carry the highest incidence. Travelers to these regions from low-incidence countries are at significant risk if they consume local food and water.

Children and adolescents in endemic regions bear the highest disease burden. Paratyphi A incidence peaks in school-aged children, likely due to increased exposure, poorer food hygiene habits, and-developing immunity.

riskFactors.factor: Consumption of street food or untreated water in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: The single greatest modifiable risk for travelers. Following strict food and water precautions — cooked food served hot, sealed bottled beverages, avoiding raw produce — reduces risk by over 90%. Travelers who adhere to these precautions during short trips to endemic regions have very low attack rates.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with a confirmed case or carrier
riskFactors.description: Household contacts of paratyphoid patients should be identified, tested (stool and blood culture), and offered post-exposure prophylaxis with azithromycin or ciprofloxacin depending on local resistance patterns.

Patients with HIV, functional asplenia, sickle cell disease, or other immunosuppressive conditions are at higher risk of severe paratyphoid infection, bacteremia, and complications. Prophylaxis may be considered in select immunocompromised travelers to high-risk regions.

Underlying biliary pathology increases the risk of chronic carriage and may complicate treatment, as Salmonella persist in gallbladders with stones. Cholecystectomy concurrent with antibiotic therapy is sometimes considered in chronic carriers with gallstones.

Patients with sickle cell disease are uniquely susceptible to Salmonella osteomyelitis and septic arthritis due to functional asplenia and areas of bone infarction. Paratyphoid C in particular has been associated with severe infections in this group.

riskFactors.factor: Working or living in institutional settings with poor sanitation
riskFactors.description: Overcrowded settings with shared sanitation are associated with outbreaks. Institutions including prisons, military barracks, and refugee camps have documented paratyphoid outbreaks linked to common-source water contamination.

affectedDemographics: In endemic regions of South Asia, children aged 5-19 years account for approximately 50-60% of reported cases, with equal sex distribution in childhood.

In high-income countries, paratyphoid presents almost exclusively in returning travelers and is rare in the indigenous population — less than 300 cases per year are reported in the United States, for example.

Travelers from high-income countries visiting friends and relatives (VFR travelers) in endemic regions carry disproportionately high risk due to longer stays, more adventurous eating patterns, and often fewer precautions than tourist travelers.
Healthcare workers caring for paratyphoid patients face occupational exposure risk, particularly in outbreak settings without adequate infection control.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of paratyphoid fever requires a high index of suspicion in any patient with a compatible illness and relevant exposure history — travel to an endemic region, household contact with a case, or institutional setting with known transmission. The definitive diagnosis is made by blood culture, which is positive in 60-80% of cases when collected before antibiotics at a volume of at least 10 mL. Bone marrow culture, though more invasive, has higher sensitivity (80-95%) and remains positive even after antibiotic therapy has begun — it is the gold standard when available. Stool culture is positive in only 30-50% of acute cases but is more useful for detecting chronic carriers. Serology — the Widal test measuring agglutinating antibodies against the O and H antigens of Salmonella Typhi and Paratyphi — is widely used in resource-limited settings because it is inexpensive and does not require laboratory infrastructure, but it lacks specificity: false positives occur with previous typhoid immunization, other Salmonella infections, and chronic liver disease. The TyphiDot rapid test and other immunochromatographic tests offer better specificity than Widal in endemic settings but are less sensitive than culture. In regions where fluoroquinolone-resistant Paratyphi A is prevalent, it is essential to inform the laboratory so that ciprofloxacin susceptibility testing is performed on all isolates. In differential diagnosis, the key distinction is from typhoid fever (both present identically), malaria (parasitemia can be confirmed or excluded on thin and thick smear), dengue (characterized by rash, thrombocytopenia, and myalgia), amoebic liver abscess (right upper quadrant pain, elevated alkaline phosphatase, positive Entamoeba serology), bacterial meningitis (CSF findings), and viral hepatitis (ALT > AST, jaundice in hepatitis A).

diagnosticTests.test: Blood culture with antibiotic susceptibility testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic test for paratyphoid fever. Should be collected before antibiotics are started whenever possible. A minimum of 10 mL of blood for adults in two separate blood culture bottles maximizes yield. The laboratory should be notified of suspected enteric fever so that appropriate culture media (typically automated blood culture systems with enrichment broth) are used.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn into two culture bottles (one aerobic, one anaerobic) and placed into an automated blood culture system. Most systems flag positive bottles within 2-5 days for enteric organisms. If negative at 5 days, a final report at 7 days is standard. Culture isolates are then forwarded for serotyping and antibiotic susceptibility testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard for definitive diagnosis with sensitivity of 80-95%, even after antibiotics have been started. Reserved for cases where blood culture is negative but clinical suspicion remains high, or for research and reference laboratory settings. Required for the most accurate serovar determination.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by a trained clinician using local anesthetic. The posterior superior iliac crest (or anterior iliac crest in children) is the preferred site. A special bone marrow aspiration needle is used to withdraw 0.5-1 mL of marrow blood. Mild soreness at the site persists for 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Useful for identifying chronic carriers and for outbreak investigation. Sensitivity in acute infection is 30-50% — lower than blood culture. A negative stool culture does not exclude active paratyphoid. Collect at least two specimens on separate days to maximize sensitivity for carrier detection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton or polyester swab inserted just past the anal sphincter. The procedure takes seconds. Samples should be placed in transport medium and cultured within 2 hours or refrigerated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures agglutinating antibodies against Salmonella Typhi O (somatic) and H (flagellar) antigens. Widely available in endemic settings due to low cost. However, sensitivity and specificity are limited: the test is positive in only 50-70% of culture-confirmed cases, and false positives occur from prior typhoid vaccination, previous infections, and other conditions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A simple blood draw. Results are typically available within hours. A four-fold rise in antibody titer between acute and convalescent samples (drawn 2-4 weeks apart) is more reliable than a single elevated result.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A point-of-care test detecting IgM antibodies against Salmonella Typhi/Paratyphi antigens. More specific than Widal, providing results in 15-20 minutes. Sensitivity is approximately 70-85% in acute infections but falls to around 50% for Paratyphi B and C. Useful in clinics without laboratory capacity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Capillary blood from a finger prick, applied to the test device with buffer. Results read at 15-20 minutes as visible test lines.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and inflammatory markers
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports the clinical picture: paratyphoid patients typically show leukopenia (low white cell count), thrombocytopenia (low platelets), and elevated C-reactive protein. Marked leukocytosis is unusual and should raise concern for a complication such as intestinal perforation or abscess.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results available same day.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and renal function panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Hepatic involvement is common in paratyphoid — transaminases are typically mildly elevated two to five times the upper limit of normal. Markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase or bilirubin should prompt investigation for amoebic liver abscess. Renal function guides antibiotic dosing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood panel drawn alongside blood cultures.

treatmentOverview: Paratyphoid fever requires prompt antibiotic therapy, which shortens the illness from 3-4 weeks to 3-7 days and prevents the complications that develop in the second and third weeks of untreated disease. The choice of antibiotic is now governed primarily by the local resistance patterns of Salmonella Paratyphi A. In South Asia, where ciprofloxacin resistance is prevalent in over 90% of Paratyphi A isolates, azithromycin (10 mg/kg/day for 7 days) or a third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone (2 g IV daily for 10-14 days) are the empiric first choices. Ciprofloxacin remains first-line in regions where susceptibility is confirmed. In severe disease requiring hospitalization, ceftriaxone is typically started intravenously and stepped down to oral azithromycin or ciprofloxacin once the patient is afebrile and clinically improving, completing a 10-14 day total course. Glucocorticoids (dexamethasone at 3 mg/kg loading, then 1 mg/kg every 6 hours for 8 doses) have been shown in randomized trials to reduce mortality in severe typhoid and paratyphoid with obtundation, shock, or coma — though this is rarely needed in contemporary practice in high-income settings where early presentation and treatment are the norm. Management of chronic carriers requires extended antibiotic courses — typically 4-6 weeks of azithromycin or ciprofloxacin — and cholecystectomy is sometimes performed concurrently in carriers with gallstones to improve cure rates. Fluid and electrolyte management is essential in patients with prolonged diarrhea, and nutritional support is important during the recovery phase. Antipyretics (paracetamol/acetaminophen) help manage fever and headache. Antidiarrheals such as loperamide should be avoided during acute enteric fever as they can prolong bacterial shedding and mask intestinal complications.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (10 mg/kg on day 1, then 5 mg/kg on days 2-7; or 500 mg daily for 7 days in adults)
medicalTreatments.description: A macrolide antibiotic with excellent intracellular penetration and proven efficacy against both ciprofloxacin-susceptible and fluoroquinolone-resistant Salmonella Paratyphi A. Resistance to azithromycin remains uncommon. First-line for uncomplicated paratyphoid in most settings, and preferred for the oral step-down phase after ceftriaxone in severe cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates of 85-95% in uncomplicated paratyphoid when susceptibility is confirmed or assumed. Effective in approximately 80% of fluoroquinolone-resistant infections.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone (2 g IV once daily for 10-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: A third-generation cephalosporin with excellent Gram-negative coverage and beta-lactamase stability. First-line for severe paratyphoid requiring hospitalization, particularly when fluoroquinolone resistance is suspected or confirmed. Can be administered intramuscularly if IV access is not feasible.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates exceed 90% for severe paratyphoid when given for 10-14 days. Relapse occurs in approximately 5-8% of cases, which may require retreatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin (500 mg twice daily for 7-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: A fluoroquinolone and historically the first-line treatment for enteric fever. Now limited by widespread fluoroquinolone resistance in Paratyphi A from South and Southeast Asia, with resistance rates above 90% in Nepal, Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, and Vietnam. In Western travelers returning from these regions, it should not be used empirically without susceptibility confirmation. Still effective in some Middle Eastern, African, and Latin American strains.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in 95%+ of ciprofloxacin-susceptible strains. In regions where resistance is prevalent, failure rates can exceed 50%.

medicalTreatments.name: Chloramphenicol (500 mg four times daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: The original 'miracle drug' for typhoid and paratyphoid from the 1940s. Now rarely used as first-line due to significant adverse effects (dose-dependent and reversible bone marrow suppression, aplastic anemia, Gray baby syndrome in neonates) and widespread resistance. Still occasionally used in resource-limited settings where newer agents are unavailable and susceptibility is confirmed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in 95%+ of susceptible strains; largely historical in contemporary practice in high-income countries.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (500/125 mg three times daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative for susceptible strains in patients who cannot tolerate other agents. Variable efficacy against MDR strains.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 80% cure rate in fully susceptible strains; less effective against MDR organisms.

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone (3 mg/kg IV loading dose, then 1 mg/kg every 6 hours for 8 doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive glucocorticoid therapy for severe paratyphoid fever with altered mental status, shock, or evidence of severe systemic inflammation. The 1986 WHO study in Javanese children showed a dramatic reduction in mortality in severe cases — from 22% to 6% with adjunctive steroids. Not indicated for uncomplicated disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality in severe enteric fever with neurologic involvement or shock when used early in the course.

surgicalOptions.name: Intestinal resection or repair for perforation
surgicalOptions.description: Intestinal perforation, most commonly occurring in the ileum over diseased Peyer's patches, requires emergent laparotomy and repair or resection. Mortality without surgery approaches 100%; with prompt surgery and appropriate antibiotics, survival ranges from 60-85% depending on patient age and comorbidities. Simple closure is preferred over resection where possible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival rates of 60-85% with prompt surgery and targeted antibiotics; mortality rises sharply with delayed presentation.

surgicalOptions.name: Cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal) for chronic carriage
surgicalOptions.description: Chronic Salmonella carriers with persistent stool shedding despite prolonged antibiotic therapy, particularly those with gallstones, may benefit from cholecystectomy combined with antibiotic therapy. This approach has been shown to reduce carriage rates significantly compared to antibiotics alone in selected patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of chronic carriage in approximately 80-90% of carriers who undergo cholecystectomy plus antibiotics, versus 50-60% with antibiotics alone in those with gallstones.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation (Saccharomyces boulardii or Lactobacillus strains)
alternativeTreatments.description: Adjunctive evidence from randomized trials suggests that probiotic strains can reduce the duration of diarrhea and bacterial shedding in Salmonella enteritis. The evidence specifically for paratyphoid is more limited, but S. boulardii has shown benefit as adjunctive therapy in enteric fever trials. Not a substitute for antibiotics.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) for fluid and electrolyte replacement
alternativeTreatments.description: Paratyphoid patients with prolonged diarrhea risk significant dehydration and electrolyte depletion, particularly hyponatremia. WHO ORS remains the cornerstone of fluid management in resource-limited settings. Adjunctive zinc supplementation is recommended for children with enteric infection.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist should be involved when the diagnosis is uncertain, when paratyphoid fever is severe (requiring hospitalization, IV antibiotics, or raising concern for complications), when initial antibiotic therapy fails (persistent fever beyond 5-7 days), when fluoroquinolone resistance is suspected or confirmed, when a chronic carrier state is identified and treatment is being planned, or when a patient with immunocompromise or hemoglobinopathy develops paratyphoid. Primary care and travel medicine providers manage diagnosis and referral for returning travelers and coordinate public health notification.
doctorSelectionGuide: For travelers, a travel medicine clinic or infectious disease specialist with experience in tropical infections is ideal. Ask whether the clinician is familiar with current local resistance patterns for enteric fever in the region visited. For chronic carriage, a specialist in infectious diseases and gastroenterology/hepatology should be seen jointly to evaluate gallbladder status and plan combined medical-surgical management. Continuity matters for carrier follow-up, which requires repeated stool cultures over 6-12 months.
preventionStrategies: Vaccination: Typhenol Vi polysaccharide vaccine or the newer conjugate vaccines (Typbar TCV) protect against Salmonella Typhi but not Paratyphi A, which now causes the majority of enteric fever cases in many regions. Travelers to high-risk regions are still at risk for paratyphoid. No paratyphoid-specific vaccine is currently widely available outside some formulations used in China and Austria (CVD 1908).

Food and water precautions: Boil all drinking water or drink only sealed bottled beverages. Eat only cooked food served piping hot. Avoid raw fruits and vegetables unless they can be peeled by the traveler. Never consume food from street vendors with questionable hygiene in endemic regions.
Hand hygiene: Regular hand washing with soap and clean water, especially before eating and after using the toilet, reduces transmission in both endemic and household settings.
Safe food sourcing: In endemic areas, avoid unpasteurized dairy products, raw or undercooked shellfish, and any food prepared with untreated water.
Post-exposure prophylaxis: Household contacts of confirmed paratyphoid cases may receive prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin or ciprofloxacin) to prevent symptomatic infection and reduce carriage.

Chronic carrier precautions: Confirmed carriers should not handle food for others, should practice meticulous hand hygiene after using the toilet, and should be referred for evaluation for cholecystectomy if gallstones are present.
lifestyleModifications: Adopt consistent food and water precautions during travel to any endemic region, even for short trips and even in higher-end accommodations where food may appear safe.
Maintain rigorous hand hygiene — wash with soap and clean water for at least 20 seconds before meals and after using the toilet — particularly in high-transmission settings.
Separate bathroom facilities for chronic carriers, with disinfectants (bleach solution) used daily to reduce environmental contamination.
Resume activities gradually after treatment — paratyphoid fatigue can persist for several weeks after the infection is cleared, and full recovery may take 6-8 weeks.
Inform healthcare providers of a history of paratyphoid infection before any future medical procedures, as it may affect choices around biliary surgery or other interventions.

recommended: Small, frequent meals as appetite returns during recovery — the gut lining regenerates over 2-4 weeks and large meals may cause discomfort early in recovery.
Easily digestible complex carbohydrates (rice, bananas, toast, boiled potatoes) during the acute phase and early recovery.
Electrolyte solutions or broths to replace sodium and potassium lost during fever and diarrhea.
Cooked vegetables and well-cooked proteins (eggs, poultry, fish) as appetite returns.
Probiotic-rich foods such as yogurt and kefir after diarrhea has resolved and antibiotics are complete — to help restore normal gut flora.
Generous fluid intake: water, oral rehydration solution, clear soups — at least 2-3 liters daily during the acute phase.
avoid: Raw or undercooked foods of any kind during travel to endemic regions — this is the most critical dietary precaution and applies to all travelers.
Unpasteurized dairy products — raw milk, raw cheese, and ice cream made from unpasteurized milk carry risk of Salmonella and other enteric pathogens.
Spicy, fried, or heavily seasoned foods during acute paratyphoid when nausea, abdominal pain, and diarrhea are present — these can exacerbate symptoms.
High-fiber foods during the acute diarrheal phase — roughage can worsen abdominal cramping and stool frequency.
Caffeinated beverages — caffeine can exacerbate the tachycardia and restlessness associated with systemic infection.
Street food from vendors with no access to clean water or refrigeration in endemic regions.

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute phase of paratyphoid fever, rest is essential — the systemic infection and fever demand significant energy, and physical activity can exacerbate complications, particularly in patients with hepatosplenomegaly. Gradual return to exercise is appropriate once the patient has been afebrile for at least one week, is tolerating normal meals, and has regained baseline energy levels. Light walking for the first two weeks of recovery, with a gradual increase in intensity, is generally well-tolerated. Contact sports and vigorous exercise should be deferred for at least 3-4 weeks from clinical recovery, particularly if hepatosplenomegaly was present on examination, to minimize the theoretical risk of splenic injury.
dailyManagement: Complete the full antibiotic course — stopping early when fever resolves (typically within 3-5 days of effective antibiotic therapy) is the most common reason for relapse.
Measure temperature twice daily during the acute phase and for at least one week after completing antibiotics — recurrent or persistent fever warrants medical review.
Stool cultures at 1 week, 1 month, and 3 months after treatment completion to confirm clearance, particularly if the patient is a food handler, healthcare worker, or caregiver.
Report any new abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or change in mental status to a clinician immediately — these may signal complications developing even during or after treatment.
Hydrate aggressively — at least 2 liters of fluid daily during the acute phase, more if diarrhea is active.
prognosis: With prompt antibiotic treatment, paratyphoid fever resolves within 5-10 days in the vast majority of patients, and mortality is below 1% in high-income healthcare settings. Complications — intestinal perforation, hemorrhage, encephalopathy, septic shock — develop primarily in patients who present late, receive inappropriate antibiotics, or have significant comorbidities. In these cases, mortality rises substantially. Long-term sequelae are uncommon in patients who recover from the acute infection. Chronic carriage occurs in approximately 1-4% of survivors and may persist for life, acting as a reservoir for ongoing transmission. The chronic carrier state carries an increased risk of biliary disease, including cholangiocarcinoma — epidemiologic studies from the Indian subcontinent and Pakistan have suggested a two to four-fold increased risk of gallbladder cancer among chronic Salmonella carriers, though causality is not definitively established.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically resolves within 3-5 days of starting an appropriate antibiotic to which the organism is susceptible. Headache and abdominal symptoms improve within 2-4 days of defervescence. Most patients feel significantly better by day 7-10 of effective treatment and can resume normal activities gradually over the following 2-4 weeks. Fatigue can persist for 6-8 weeks, particularly after severe infections. Stool cultures should be monitored for clearance at 1, 4, and 12 weeks post-treatment to confirm the carrier state has not developed. Chronic carriers may require months of treatment and, in some cases, gallbladder surgery to achieve microbiologic clearance.
complications: Intestinal perforation — the most feared complication, occurring in 1-3% of hospitalized cases in the pre-antibiotic era and remaining a significant cause of mortality in delayed presentations. Perforations are typically small and singular, located in the ileum over necrotic Peyer's patches.
Intestinal hemorrhage — occurs from ulcerated Peyer's patches in the second or third week; manifests as rectal bleeding, melena, and falling hemoglobin. Often coexists with perforation.

Encephalopathy and delirium — altered mental status in severe paratyphoid results from meningitis, hyponatremia, or the systemic inflammatory response. Associated with high mortality without adjunctive steroids.
Myocarditis — inflammation of the heart muscle, presenting with chest pain, ECG changes, and elevated cardiac enzymes. Usually reversible with appropriate antibiotics and supportive care.

Chronic carrier state — persists in 1-4% of patients after clinical recovery, with shedding in stool for over one year. Chronic carriers have an increased risk of biliary malignancy and require monitoring and, in many cases, treatment.

Osteomyelitis and septic arthritis — Salmonella has a unique tropism for infarcted bone, making patients with sickle cell disease particularly vulnerable. Any focal bone or joint pain in a paratyphoid patient warrants urgent imaging.
Endocarditis and suppurative thrombophlebitis — rare but serious, occurring in patients with underlying valvular disease or IV drug use.

supportResources.name: CDC Yellow Book — Typhoid and Paratyphoid Fever
supportResources.description: Up-to-date US guidance on typhoid and paratyphoid fever for healthcare professionals and travelers, including vaccination recommendations and antibiotic resistance maps.

supportResources.name: WHO Typhoid and Paratyphoid Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: Global surveillance data, treatment guidelines, and public health recommendations from the World Health Organization.

supportResources.name: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary — Typhoid Fever
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline covering diagnosis and management of typhoid and paratyphoid fever for primary care practitioners.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Caused by Salmonella Typhi rather than Paratyphi serovars. The clinical presentation is identical — both cause the classic enteric fever syndrome of prolonged stepwise fever, headache, constipation, abdominal pain, rose spots, and hepatosplenomegaly. Differentiation requires blood culture with serotyping. Both respond to similar antibiotics, though Typhi historically had slightly different resistance patterns. Typhi is covered by Vi polysaccharide and conjugate vaccines; Paratyphi A is not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes in endemic regions. Presents with cyclic fevers, chills, rigors, headache, and sometimes hepatosplenomegaly. Positive thin and thick blood smears and rapid antigen tests confirm malaria. Co-infection with enteric fever is possible and should be excluded in febrile travelers from malaria-endemic areas. Typhoid does not respond to antimalarial drugs and vice versa.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A mosquito-borne viral hemorrhagic fever endemic in tropical urban areas overlapping with paratyphoid regions. Presents with high fever, severe retro-orbital headache, myalgia, arthralgia, and a maculopapular rash appearing on day 3-4. Thrombocytopenia and hemoconcentration are characteristic. No antibiotic response. Confirmed by NS1 antigen test, IgM ELISA, or PCR.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amoebic Dysentery (Entamoeba histolytica)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Intestinal amebiasis presents with bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and tenesmus rather than the systemic prolonged fever of paratyphoid. Amoebic liver abscess, a complication of intestinal infection, causes right upper quadrant pain, fever, and a characteristic single large hepatic lesion on ultrasound. Amebic serology and stool ova-and-parasite examination confirm the diagnosis. Metronidazole or tinidazole is the treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Salmonella Typhi causes the clinically identical typhoid fever syndrome. Both are enteric fevers spread by the fecal-oral route, share risk factors, occur in the same endemic regions, and are treated with similar antibiotic regimens — though Typhi and Paratyphi frequently have different resistance profiles locally. Vaccination against Typhoid does not protect against Paratyphi A.

relatedConditions.name: Salmonella Infection (Non-Typhoidal)
relatedConditions.relevance: Non-typhoidal Salmonella (S. Enteritidis, S. Typhimurium) causes self-limited gastroenteritis rather than systemic enteric fever and is treated differently (supportive care; antibiotics only in immunocompromised). Genetic and microbiologic comparisons inform understanding of paratyphoid pathogenesis and immune response.

relatedConditions.name: Foodborne Illness
relatedConditions.relevance: Paratyphoid fever is a foodborne illness in the broad sense, contracted through contaminated food and water. All foodborne illness prevention guidance applies to paratyphoid prevention.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Malaria and paratyphoid share overlapping endemic geographies in sub-Saharan Africa, where co-infection is well-documented and presents diagnostic and therapeutic challenges — both can coexist in the same febrile patient.

faqs.question: What is paratyphoid fever and how is it different from typhoid fever?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever is a systemic bacterial infection caused by Salmonella enterica serovars Paratyphi A, B, and C. It is clinically indistinguishable from typhoid fever, which is caused by Salmonella Typhi. Both produce the same syndrome of prolonged fever, headache, abdominal pain, constipation or diarrhea, rose spots, and hepatosplenomegaly. The only reliable way to distinguish them is through blood culture with serotyping. Both are spread by the fecal-oral route through contaminated food and water, and both are treated with similar antibiotics, though resistance patterns differ. The key practical difference is that the Vi typhoid vaccine does not protect against paratyphoid fever.

faqs.question: How do you get paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever is contracted by swallowing Salmonella Paratyphi bacteria in contaminated food or water. The bacteria pass through the stomach, invade the intestinal lining, and spread through the bloodstream to the liver, spleen, and other organs. Common sources in endemic areas include untreated drinking water, ice made from contaminated water, raw fruits and vegetables washed in contaminated water, unpasteurized dairy products, and food prepared by an infected carrier. Person-to-person transmission also occurs through inadequate hand hygiene after using the toilet.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: The early symptoms (first week) include gradually rising fever, severe headache, generalized malaise, abdominal pain and tenderness, and often constipation in adults. By the second week, fever is typically high and sustained, diarrhea may appear, the liver and spleen enlarge, and some patients develop faint rose-colored spots on the trunk. Without treatment, the third week can bring worsening abdominal pain (concerning for intestinal perforation), bleeding, confusion, or worsening systemic illness. Some patients develop a dry cough early in the illness.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: With prompt and appropriate antibiotic treatment, fever typically resolves within 3-5 days and most symptoms improve within 7-10 days. The full antibiotic course should be completed even after fever resolves, to prevent relapse. Most patients feel back to normal within 2-4 weeks after starting effective treatment, though lingering fatigue is common for 6-8 weeks, particularly after severe infections. Patients with complications such as intestinal perforation require significantly longer hospitalization and recovery.

faqs.question: Can paratyphoid fever be treated without antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever requires antibiotic treatment. Without it, the illness lasts 3-4 weeks and carries a significant risk of serious complications including intestinal perforation (in 1-3% of untreated cases), hemorrhage, encephalopathy, and death. Antipyretics and hydration can manage symptoms and prevent dehydration, but they do not eliminate the infection. Once the diagnosis is confirmed or strongly suspected, appropriate antibiotics should be started without delay. Starting antibiotics before a definitive diagnosis can reduce culture sensitivity, but this trade-off is clinically appropriate given the risks of untreated disease.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: There is no widely available paratyphoid-specific vaccine. The typhoid vaccines (Vi polysaccharide vaccine injected, Ty21a oral vaccine, and the newer Vi-conjugate vaccine) protect against Salmonella Typhi but not against Paratyphi A, B, or C. A paratyphoid vaccine candidate (CVD 1908) has shown promising results in field trials but is not yet commercially available in most countries. Travelers to highly endemic regions where paratyphoid is common should receive the typhoid vaccine as a partial protective measure and follow strict food and water precautions, recognizing that this covers only part of the enteric fever risk.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used to treat paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: The first-line antibiotics depend on local resistance patterns. Azithromycin (an oral macrolide) and ceftriaxone (a third-generation cephalosporin given IV) are the most widely reliable options globally and are effective against fluoroquinolone-resistant strains that are common in South and Southeast Asia. Ciprofloxacin (a fluoroquinolone) was the historical first choice but is now ineffective in many regions due to widespread fluoroquinolone resistance in Paratyphi A. Chloramphenicol, ampicillin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole were used historically but are now largely ineffective due to multidrug resistance. The total treatment course is typically 7-14 days depending on the antibiotic and severity.

faqs.question: Why is paratyphoid fever becoming harder to treat?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever is increasingly difficult to treat because of antimicrobial resistance. Salmonella Paratyphi A in South Asia has developed resistance to multiple first-line drugs: the classical MDR profile (resistance to chloramphenicol, ampicillin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) emerged in the 1980s, and fluoroquinolone resistance via mutations in the gyrA gene became prevalent in the 1990s and 2000s, making ciprofloxacin ineffective in most isolates from India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and much of Southeast Asia. This leaves azithromycin and third-generation cephalosporins as the main effective oral and IV options. Azithromycin resistance has now been detected in parts of Southeast Asia, which is a concerning development that clinicians are monitoring closely.

faqs.question: Can you die from paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: With prompt diagnosis and appropriate antibiotic treatment, paratyphoid fever has a mortality rate below 1% in high-income healthcare settings. Without treatment, or with delayed treatment due to missed diagnosis, mortality rises to 10-15%, with deaths primarily caused by intestinal perforation, hemorrhage, severe encephalopathy, or septic shock. Deaths are most common in young children, older adults, and patients with significant comorbidities or immunocompromise in resource-limited settings. Even in endemic regions, early presentation and treatment dramatically changes the prognosis.

faqs.question: How is paratyphoid fever diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The diagnosis is confirmed by blood culture, which is positive in 60-80% of cases when collected before antibiotics at adequate volume. Bone marrow culture has higher sensitivity (80-95%) and is used when blood culture is negative but clinical suspicion remains high. Stool culture can identify the organism but is less sensitive in acute infection. Serology (the Widal test) is widely used in resource-limited settings because it is inexpensive, but it can be falsely positive from prior typhoid vaccination or infection. Rapid immunochromatographic tests offer better specificity than Widal. Blood cultures should always be collected before antibiotics if possible.

faqs.question: What is a chronic carrier of paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: A chronic carrier is someone who continues to shed Salmonella Paratyphi in their stool for more than one year after recovering from the acute illness. The bacteria persist in the gallbladder, protected by biofilm formation on gallstones and biliary epithelium. Approximately 1-4% of people who recover from paratyphoid become chronic carriers. They usually feel completely well but can transmit the infection to others through food handling and poor hand hygiene. Chronic carriers are at increased risk of biliary disease. Carriage can usually be cleared with 4-6 weeks of antibiotic therapy; if gallstones are present, cholecystectomy combined with antibiotics is often more effective than antibiotics alone.

faqs.question: Is paratyphoid fever contagious?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever is contagious through the fecal-oral route. An infected person sheds bacteria in their stool (and occasionally urine) during the acute illness and, in some cases, for months to years as a chronic carrier. Casual contact is unlikely to transmit infection; transmission requires ingesting contaminated food or water. Close household contacts, people sharing bathroom facilities without proper hygiene, and food handlers who are carriers are the main transmission risks. With appropriate hygiene and completion of treatment, the risk of ongoing transmission from a treated case is low.

faqs.question: Who is at highest risk for paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: Children aged 5-15 years in endemic regions carry the highest burden of paratyphoid fever, accounting for the majority of cases in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa. Travelers to endemic regions from high-income countries are the next highest-risk group, particularly those visiting friends and relatives (VFR travelers) who tend to have longer stays and less adherence to food precautions. People with functional asplenia (from sickle cell disease or other conditions), HIV, or other immunocompromising conditions face higher risk of severe infection and complications. Food handlers working without adequate sanitation in endemic regions also face elevated occupational risk.

faqs.question: How can I prevent paratyphoid fever when traveling?
faqs.answer: For travelers to endemic regions, the most effective prevention is food and water caution: drink only sealed bottled or boiled water, eat only cooked food served hot, avoid raw fruits and vegetables and street food in uncertain conditions, and wash hands frequently with clean water. The typhoid vaccine (Vi polysaccharide or conjugate injection) protects against Typhoid but not Paratyphi A or B — it reduces overall enteric fever risk by roughly 50-80% depending on the vaccine. Post-exposure prophylaxis with azithromycin may be considered for close household contacts of confirmed cases. No paratyphoid-specific vaccine is currently widely available.

faqs.question: What complications can paratyphoid fever cause?
faqs.answer: The most serious complications occur in the second and third weeks of untreated disease: intestinal perforation (causing sudden severe abdominal pain and peritonitis), intestinal hemorrhage (causing bloody stools and falling blood pressure), and encephalopathy or delirium. Other complications include myocarditis (heart muscle inflammation), osteomyelitis (bone infection, especially in sickle cell patients), septic arthritis, endocarditis, and chronic carriage. With prompt antibiotic treatment, complications are uncommon in high-income settings — fewer than 5% of appropriately treated patients develop them.

faqs.question: Is paratyphoid fever the same as salmonella poisoning?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever is caused by Salmonella bacteria, but it is not the same as the more common Salmonella food poisoning (non-typhoidal salmonellosis caused by S. Enteritidis, S. Typhimurium, and similar serovars). Non-typhoidal Salmonella causes acute gastroenteritis with diarrhea, vomiting, and cramping that typically resolves within a week without antibiotics. Paratyphoid fever, by contrast, is a systemic enteric fever with prolonged high fever, headache, and organ involvement that requires antibiotics and carries a risk of serious complications. The two conditions are related microbiologically but distinct clinically and in their treatment approach.

faqs.question: Can paratyphoid fever come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse occurs in approximately 5-8% of patients who complete an appropriate antibiotic course, typically within 2-3 weeks of apparent recovery. Relapse is more common when the antibiotic course is too short, when an inadequate volume of antibiotics is used, or when the infecting strain has partial antibiotic resistance. Relapse is treated by restarting an appropriate antibiotic course, often for a longer duration than the first treatment. To minimize relapse risk, complete the full prescribed course even after fever and symptoms resolve, and confirm microbiologic clearance with follow-up stool cultures.

faqs.question: What should I do if a family member has paratyphoid fever?
faqs.answer: If a household member is diagnosed with paratyphoid fever, other household members should seek medical evaluation — blood and stool cultures — even if they feel well, as asymptomatic infection and early infection are both possible. Household contacts may be offered post-exposure prophylaxis with azithromycin depending on local guidelines. Strict hygiene is essential: separate bathroom facilities if possible, bleach-based disinfectants for toilet surfaces, meticulous hand washing with soap after each toilet visit and before food preparation, and no food handling by the patient or infected contacts until cleared. All contaminated clothing and linens should be washed in hot water.

faqs.question: How is paratyphoid fever treated in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever in pregnancy requires prompt antibiotic treatment, as untreated enteric fever poses a significant risk to both mother and fetus including miscarriage, preterm labor, and maternal sepsis. Ceftriaxone (a third-generation cephalosporin) is considered safe in pregnancy and is the preferred choice for moderate-to-severe paratyphoid in pregnant patients. Azithromycin is generally considered acceptable in pregnancy when needed, though it is not a first-line choice for most non-pregnant patients. Fluoroquinolones are generally avoided in pregnancy due to theoretical concerns about cartilage development, but are sometimes used when no safer alternatives are available for MDR infections. Treatment should never be delayed pending culture confirmation in a febrile pregnant traveler.

faqs.question: Is paratyphoid fever common in the United States or Europe?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever is rare in high-income countries like the United States, United Kingdom, and most of Western Europe, where fewer than 300 cases per year are reported in the US and equivalent numbers in the UK. Almost all cases in these countries occur in returning travelers from endemic regions — particularly South Asia, Southeast Asia, and sub-Saharan Africa. Indigenous transmission is extremely uncommon due to water sanitation infrastructure and food safety standards. Travelers to endemic regions should be aware of the risk and take appropriate precautions, including typhoid vaccination.

faqs.question: What is the difference between paratyphoid fever and amoebic dysentery?
faqs.answer: Paratyphoid fever is a systemic bacterial infection causing prolonged high fever, headache, and abdominal pain, diagnosed by blood culture and treated with antibiotics. Amoebic dysentery is an intestinal infection with the parasite Entamoeba histolytica, causing bloody diarrhea with mucus, abdominal cramping, and tenesmus — with diarrhea typically more prominent than fever. Amoebic liver abscess, a complication, causes right upper quadrant pain and fever with a single large liver lesion on imaging. The two conditions can coexist and should both be considered in febrile travelers with abdominal symptoms from endemic regions. Treatment for amebiasis is metronidazole or tinidazole, completely different from paratyphoid antibiotics.

faqs.question: Do I need follow-up tests after paratyphoid fever treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — follow-up stool cultures are essential to confirm that the infection has been cleared, particularly if you are a food handler, healthcare worker, childcare provider, or live with someone in a high-risk group. Collect at least three stool specimens on separate days at 1 week, 1 month, and 3 months after completing treatment. If any of these cultures is positive, you may have a chronic carrier state that requires extended treatment and possibly gallbladder evaluation. Blood tests are not useful for confirming cure — stool culture is the standard.

metaDescription: Paratyphoid fever symptoms, causes, and treatment with azithromycin or ceftriaxone for travelers to South Asia. CDC & WHO reviewed.
